He has the ideal, projectable body of a pitcher, thanks to his angular build and lean muscle. Scouts note that he has a fluid, easy arm action and a smooth downhill delivery. The southpaw looks the part and is a presence on the mound. Cornwell has a two-seam fastball that touches 90 mph and has sink, and the pitch can produce ground balls when he keeps it low in the zone.

After his freshman year at Florida, Schwarz seemed like a sure-fire top-of-the-Draft prospect for 2017. An inconsistent 2016 season, combined with a lack of work behind the plate, leaves his status a little bit more up in the air. The son of one-time big league pitcher Jeff Schwarz, J.J. still has tremendous offensive potential. He's shown he has raw power to all fields. While there was swing-and-miss to his game in 2016, he also showed a willingness to work walks and get on base. He has the tools to catch, with good hands and a solid average arm, but he hasn't, and won't, catch that much because of Mike Rivera's presence on the Gators. Schwarz went to the Cape last summer to catch, but he left after 13 games due to a minor injury. If Schwarz gets some time behind the plate and shows he can catch, that will obviously help his value. But even if not, if his bat returns to freshman form, he could be one of the first college position player to come off the board.

school.name: St. Johns River State JC, FL

picks.blurb: DeLuca, in addition to playing baseball, participates in track and field in the spring and recorded one of the top long jumps in the state of California as a senior. One of the best all-around athletes in the draft, the Oregon commit possesses above-average contact skills from both sides of the plate. His speed is a major asset on the bases and allows him to cover a lot of ground in the outfield to complement his plus arm.

Leftwich spent two years at Winter Park High School in the Orlando, Fla., area before switching his academics to the online Florida Virtual School and his baseball to the TNXL Academy. He finished his coursework to graduate last fall, giving him months to focus on baseball full-time to prepare for life as a pro or as another talented arm at the University of Florida. The work allowed Leftwich to get his body in shape and helped him show off his arm strength more consistently. He's capable of running his fastball up to 96-97 mph, especially in shorter stints. His breaking ball will flash average, but it will often get caught between a curveball and a slider with a 10-to-4-type break, though at times he'll go to a more conventional slider, and he does have some feel for a changeup. While he throws strikes with his heater, its lack of movement makes it play down a bit. He can be inverted in back during his delivery, impacting his plane. As a result, some see a future reliever profile, though he has shown the stuff and control to start. The biggest thing that might keep Leftwich from going in the top six rounds is a rumored price tag, with him donning a Gators uniform in the fall a distinct possibility.

Dobzanski is an athletic high schooler with a clean delivery who projects as a reliever down the road. His fastball sits between 86-90 mph, and his breaking ball is sharp at 77-82 mph. Both pitches are accentuated by a cross-body delivery that hides ball from hitters.

With a more developed frame and better velocity on his pitches, Glick has drawn the interest of scouts. He's tall, but lean with muscle and steadily improving. On the mound, he exhibits fluid, clean arm action and a balanced, downhill delivery that looks easy. He has a crafty three-pitch arsenal and feel for a nearly average curveball. Overall, scouts have say that the UCLA commit has a high ceiling and has the looks of a high-round Draft selection. Glick attended MLB's Prospect Development Pipeline premier event in Southern California in February.

Fariss intrigued scouts on the mound. He has a compact arm and gets some deception out of his delivery. At times, he will rush pitches and drop his arm, but his fastball was average with some late life and his breaking ball has noticeable potential. Farris attended MLB's Prospect Development Pipeline premier event in Lake Elsinore, Calif., in February.

Jenkins, a South Carolina commit, was a member of a Collins Hill High School team that went 34-2 and was ranked as the 8th-best high school roster in the nation last season. A very projectable outfielder, Jenkins has a solid, strong frame and average arm strength in the field. He boasts noticeable bat speed, and he can make consistent contact even on inside pitches. Jenkins attended MLB's Prospect Development Pipeline premier event in Atlanta in January.

A star quarterback, Radcliff had football offers from Atlantic Coast Conference and Southeastern Conference schools but is committed to baseball. He offers some of the best left-handed power upside in this Draft class, though the rest of his game is raw enough that teams aren't sure he'll go high enough to deter him from attending Georgia Tech. At 6-foot-3 and 220 pounds, Radcliff already has a big league body. With his size, strength, leverage and bat speed, he drives balls a long way when he barrels them up. That doesn't happen consistently, because he lacks timing and tries to do too much at the plate, lengthening his swing and getting pull-happy. While Radcliff has posted sub-6.6-second times in the 60-yard dash, he's quicker in a straight line and plays more as an average runner on the diamond. His outfield instincts need a lot of polish and he has fringy arm strength, so he projects as a left fielder.

Cantrelle, a star at Teurlings Catholic, led his Rebels to an appearance in the Louisiana state finals as a sophomore pitcher and shortstop. After receiving interest from top programs like Cal State Fullerton and LSU, Cantrelle committed to play for Louisiana-Lafayette. A versatile player, Cantrelle showcases skills from both sides of the plate. He displays good bat control and a smooth swing with line-drive power to the gaps as both a left-handed and right-handed hitter. Cantrelle attended the MLB Prospect Development Pipeline premier event in Houston in January.

Anderson has a wide array of tools but scouts aren't quite sure what to make of him. He performed well on the showcase circuit last summer but has missed time this spring with oblique and leg injuries. It's unclear whether he'll get picked early enough to deter him from attending Mississippi State, though it's possible a team willing to be patient with his development could bite as early as the third round. A streaky hitter, Anderson can barrel balls against quality pitching at times and try to do too much at others. He looks physically stronger this spring and could have 15-homer upside, but his right-handed swing gets long when he thinks about driving the ball and he has trouble with breaking pitches. While he has solid bat speed, there are some concerns about his feel for hitting and how much of his pop he'll be able to tap into. Anderson consistently recorded well above-average run times last summer but hasn't been as quick or healthy as a senior. He's still honing his defensive instincts, leading some evaluators to wonder if he might eventually have to move from center field to a corner. His strong arm will play anywhere in the outfield.
